module.title: Module 6: Water Rights & Water Boundaries
module.description: Riparian and littoral rights, navigability, ownership of streambeds, and the location of water boundaries — taught Texas-style around the gradient boundary, the 30-foot navigable-stream statute, the dual coastal datum, and the rule of capture.
learning_objectives: Differentiate between riparian rights (flowing watercourses) and littoral rights (lakes, seas, the Gulf), and explain why a riparian boundary must exist before any such rights attach.

Trace the historical evolution of water rights from Roman civil law and English common law through the U.S. equal-footing doctrine to the Texas sovereign-grant system, and explain how that history shaped private and public ownership of beds and shores.

Locate Texas water boundaries: apply the statutory 30-foot test of navigability (Tex. Nat. Res. Code §21.001), the gradient boundary on navigable streams (Motl v. Boyd; Oklahoma v. Texas), the medium-filum-aquae rule on non-navigable streams, and the dual tidal datum on the coast (Luttes v. State).

Compare and contrast the effects of shoreline change — accretion, reliction, erosion, and avulsion — on title and on the location of a water boundary, and apportion newly formed riparian land among adjoining owners.
Distinguish Texas surface-water law (State ownership, TCEQ appropriation permits) from Texas groundwater law (rule of capture, ownership in place under Edwards Aquifer Authority v. Day).

lessons.title: Riparian vs. Littoral Rights and the Historical Roots of Water Law
learning_objectives: Define riparian and littoral rights and identify which applies to a given body of water.
Explain how Roman, Spanish, and English common law fed into American — and specifically Texas — water-boundary law.
Apply the equal-footing doctrine and the four-sovereign Texas chain of title to questions of bed ownership.

content.html:
Water as a boundary monument

Natural water features — rivers, creeks, lakes, bays, and the Gulf — have served as ownership boundaries since the earliest land grants. Because water is a natural monument, it sits at the top of the dignity-of-calls hierarchy and controls over course, distance, and area. But a water boundary is unusual: it can move, the land beneath it may be owned by someone other than the upland owner, and the public may hold rights in the water even where a private party owns the bed. This module untangles those layers.

The vocabulary comes from Latin. A riparian owner (from ripa, river bank) owns land along the bank of a watercourse — a river, stream, or, in common usage, a lake. A littoral owner (from litus, seashore) owns land abutting a sea or ocean where the tide rises and falls. Surveyors and courts often use "riparian" loosely to cover both, but the precise distinction matters: the boundary rules and the public-trust burdens differ between flowing water and tidal water.

rows: Flowing watercourses — rivers, streams (and by usage, lakes)
Center/thread if non-navigable; gradient boundary (bank) if navigable in Texas
Static/tidal bodies — ocean, sea, Gulf, large lakes
Tidal high-water line (datum depends on the sovereign source of the grant in Texas)

content.text: No riparian boundary, no riparian rights. Riparian and littoral rights attach ONLY to a parcel that actually has a water boundary. A back lot that does not touch the water has no riparian rights — and if erosion later makes it riparian, its rights begin only then.

content.html:
From Roman law to the Crown to the States

The idea that the sovereign owns the beds of navigable waters is ancient. The Roman Civil Code (c. 500 AD) declared the beds of seas and rivers public property. Spanish law — Las Siete Partidas (1265, compiled c. 1350) — gave the King ownership of the ocean bed up to the "highest wash of the waves in winter," leaving private property only above that line. This winter-high-water concept is the seed of the Texas dual coastal datum you will meet in Lesson 4. In England, Lord Matthew Hale's treatise De Jure Maris (c. 1666) placed the foreshore — the strip overflowed by ordinary tides — in the Crown.

Under English common law the Crown held title to the soil under tidewaters but the public's rights of navigation and fishing could not be conveyed away. After the Revolution, the thirteen original colonies became sovereign successors to the Crown and took title to the beds of waters where the tide ebbed and flowed and to the beds of inland navigable waters. With the Constitution they ceded to the United States only the power to regulate interstate commerce and navigation — not title to the beds.

sections.title: The equal-footing doctrine
sections.content: When new states entered the Union after 1789 they were admitted on an equal footing with the original states. Under Pollard v. Hagan, 44 U.S. 212 (1845), each new state therefore took title to the beds of its navigable waters as of the date of statehood, subject to federal regulation of commerce. The federal government, when it sold western public lands, reserved the navigable waters for the future states.

sections.title: Seven prior sovereigns — and why Texas is different
sections.content: In the continental United States, seven prior sovereigns issued land grants: England, France, Spain, Holland, Russia, Mexico, and Texas. In the public-land states the United States was the grantor and reserved navigable waters to the states under equal footing. Texas is the exception: it entered the Union in 1845 retaining its own public domain. Texas land was never federal public domain, so a Texas water-boundary question traces back not to a federal patent but to one of four sovereigns of the soil — Spain, Mexico, the Republic of Texas, or the State of Texas — and the choice of boundary rule can turn on which one issued the grant.

content.text: Texas differs: Because Texas retained its public lands at annexation (1845) and was never on the federal PLSS, you must identify the SOVEREIGN SOURCE and DATE of the original grant before choosing a water-boundary rule. The same physical shoreline can be located differently for a Spanish/Mexican grant than for a Republic/State grant.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Riparian rights = flowing water (river/stream); littoral rights = static/tidal water (lake/sea/Gulf).
Riparian/littoral rights attach only to a parcel that actually borders the water — no water boundary, no rights.
The sovereign-owns-the-bed idea runs from Roman law through Spanish Siete Partidas and English common law into the American states via the equal-footing doctrine.

States took bed title at statehood; the U.S. reserved navigable waters when selling public lands — but Texas kept its own public domain and traces title to four sovereigns (Spain, Mexico, Republic, State).

flashcards.definition: Rights, relative to the use of moving water (river or stream), that accrue to the owner of upland bordering that watercourse.
Rights, relative to non-moving or tidal water (ocean, sea, Gulf, lake), that accrue to the owner of upland bordering that body.
New states are admitted on an equal footing with the original states and therefore take title to the beds of their navigable waters as of the date of statehood (Pollard v. Hagan, 1845).
Every Texas title traces to Spain, Mexico, the Republic of Texas, or the State of Texas — Texas land was never federal public domain.

images_needed.caption: Side-by-side schematic: a riparian lot on a flowing stream vs. a littoral lot on a tidal shore, labeling the watercourse, the upland, and the differing boundary lines.
Timeline of water-law sovereignty: Roman Civil Code, Siete Partidas (winter high water), English common law / De Jure Maris, U.S. equal-footing doctrine, and the four Texas sovereigns.

lessons.title: Navigability and Ownership of Beds: The Texas 30-Foot Statute
learning_objectives: Apply the federal commerce test and the Texas statutory 30-foot test of navigability.
State who owns the bed of a navigable vs. a non-navigable stream in Texas.
Explain the curative effect — and the mineral reservation — of the 1929 Small Bill.

content.html:
Why navigability is the threshold question

Before you can locate a water boundary or decide who owns the streambed, you must classify the water as navigable or non-navigable. The consequences are large. On a navigable stream the State of Texas owns the bed, holding it in trust for the public, and private title stops at the bank (the gradient boundary, Lesson 3). On a non-navigable stream the abutting owners take title to the center of the channel and there is no State-owned bed.

American courts long ago abandoned the old English rule that only tidal waters were navigable. In The Daniel Ball, 77 U.S. 557 (1870), the U.S. Supreme Court held that waters navigable in fact — usable as highways of commerce — are navigable in law, tide or no tide. That is the federal test: it looks at the waterway's condition around the time of statehood and asks whether it was, or was susceptible of being, used for commerce.

Texas adds a bright-line geometric test

Texas keeps the commerce test but supplements it with a unique statutory rule. Tex. Nat. Res. Code §21.001(3) defines a "navigable stream" as one that "retains an average width of 30 feet from its mouth up." The 30 feet is measured across the entire bed — from cut bank to cut bank — not the water flowing on any given day. So a creek that is dry for part of the year can still be legally navigable, and therefore public with a State-owned bed, purely because its average bed width is 30 feet or more.

The rule originates in an 1837 act of the Republic of Texas that instructed surveyors not to cross such streams when laying out grants (so the bed would stay public). It carried into the old Revised Statutes and was recodified into §21.001. Because the test is geometric, a surveyor can often answer the navigability question with a tape and a calculation — frequently using historic vara measurements from the original GLO field notes.

content.label: Statutory 30-ft navigability test (1 vara = 33⅓ in = 2.77778 ft)

content.text: Texas differs: Most states determine navigability ONLY by the commerce/navigable-in-fact test. Texas layers a bright-line statutory rule on top — average bed width of 30 feet (§21.001(3)). A small, non-commercial Texas creek can be "statutorily navigable," making its bed State land, on width alone.

sections.title: Ownership of beds — the rule in one line
sections.content: Navigable stream: the State of Texas owns the bed (in trust for the public); private patents run only to the gradient boundary. Non-navigable stream: the abutting owners own to the center/thread of the channel. Authority: Motl v. Boyd, 116 Tex. 82, 286 S.W. 458 (1926); Brainard v. State, 12 S.W.3d 6 (Tex. 2000); Tex. Nat. Res. Code Ch. 21.

sections.title: The 1929 "Small Bill" — a Texas curative statute
sections.content: Many early Texas surveys and patents crossed streambeds, clouding title once the gradient-boundary cases established that the State owned navigable beds. The 1929 "Small Bill" (historically Art. 5414a, Vernon's Rev. Civ. Stat.) relinquished and quitclaimed the State's interest in the BED SURFACE to the adjoining patentees/riparian owners and let owners short on upland make up acreage from the bed. But it reserved the minerals (and sand/gravel) to the State and expressly preserved the public's and the State's rights in the WATER. So along a navigable Texas stream the adjoining owner may now hold and pay taxes on the streambed surface, yet the public keeps navigation/recreation rights in the water and the State keeps the minerals.

content.text: Texas differs: The Small Bill (1929) is a Texas-specific curative statute born of the 1920s oil boom. Its hallmark — conveying the streambed SURFACE to private owners while SEVERING and RESERVING the minerals to the State — has no general common-law analog and still governs streambed mineral ownership in Texas title work.

links.title: Tex. Nat. Res. Code §21.001
links.description: Definitions, including the "navigable stream" 30-foot average-width rule.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Federal test: navigable-in-fact for commerce (The Daniel Ball). Texas ALSO uses a statutory test.
Tex. Nat. Res. Code §21.001(3): a stream is navigable if it retains an average bed width of 30 feet from the mouth up — measured bank to bank, not by the water present on a given day.
Navigable stream → State owns the bed (gradient boundary limits private title); non-navigable stream → abutting owners take to the center/thread.
The 1929 Small Bill relinquished the streambed SURFACE to riparian owners but reserved minerals to the State and preserved the public's water rights.

flashcards.definition: Under Tex. Nat. Res. Code §21.001(3), a stream that retains an average width of 30 feet from its mouth up — measured across the entire bed, not the day's flow.
flashcards.hint_1: A geometric, bright-line rule unique to Texas.

flashcards.definition: The federal test: waters usable, or susceptible of being used, as highways of commerce are navigable in law regardless of tide.
The State of Texas owns the bed of a navigable stream in trust for the public; private title runs only to the gradient boundary.

Texas curative statute (Art. 5414a) relinquishing the State's interest in navigable streambed SURFACE to adjoining owners while reserving minerals/sand/gravel to the State and preserving public water rights.

images_needed.caption: Stream cross-section showing average bed width measured cut-bank to cut-bank, with a 30-ft dimension line and a note that the test ignores the day's water level.
Two plan-view panels: navigable stream (State owns bed, private to gradient boundary) vs. non-navigable stream (owners to center/thread).

lessons.title: The Texas Gradient Boundary on Navigable Streams
learning_objectives: Define the gradient boundary and the qualified/cut bank.
Trace the development of the method through Oklahoma v. Texas, Motl v. Boyd, and Brainard v. State.
Contrast the gradient boundary with the ordinary high-water mark used in other states, and apply the medium-filum rule on non-navigable streams.

content.html:
Texas's most distinctive boundary

Once a stream is navigable and the State owns the bed, exactly where is the line between State bed and private upland? Most states answer "the ordinary high-water mark" — an observable vegetation or scour line. Texas does not. Texas locates the limit at the gradient boundary: a graded line along the bank lying midway between (i) the lower level of the flowing water that just reaches the foot of the cut bank, and (ii) the higher level that the water reaches without overtopping the cut bank. It is a gradient — a line that follows the slope of the bank along the stream — tied to the cut bank (also called the qualified or acceptable bank), not to a single high-water stain.

The cut bank is the relatively permanent, water-washed bank that separates the bed from the upland. Locating the gradient boundary is a specialized expert survey: it requires observing water levels, profiling the qualified bank, and interpolating a graded line — it is emphatically not a simple high-water line you can flag by eye.

content.label: Gradient boundary elevation at a station = midway between the lower water level reaching the cut bank and the higher level not overtopping it

sections.title: Oklahoma v. Texas, 260 U.S. 606 (1923)
sections.content: The Red River boundary dispute that produced the method. To fix the Texas–Oklahoma line, the U.S. Supreme Court's commissioner/surveyor Arthur A. Stiles developed the gradient boundary — a "water-washed and relatively permanent elevation" at the outer line of the river bed. Stiles later wrote the classic article The Gradient Boundary — The Line Between Texas and Oklahoma Along the Red River, 30 Tex. L. Rev. (1950).

sections.title: Motl v. Boyd, 116 Tex. 82, 286 S.W. 458 (1926)
sections.content: The Texas Supreme Court adopted the gradient boundary as the division between public and private ownership along navigable streams. It defined the "bed" as the soil "alternately covered and left bare ... adequate to contain [the water] at its average and mean stage during an entire year, without reference to the extra freshets ... or the extreme droughts." This "average and mean annual stage" idea is central: extreme floods and extreme dry spells are ignored.

sections.title: Brainard v. State, 12 S.W.3d 6 (Tex. 2000)
sections.content: The leading modern application (Canadian River). The Court restated and refined the location method, describing the gradient boundary as the mid-height point of the lowest qualified ("acceptable") bank in the vicinity. Diversion Lake Club v. Heath, 126 Tex. 129 (1935), had earlier endorsed the gradient boundary as well.

content.text: Texas differs (the single most distinctive concept in this module): Virtually every other state fixes the riparian boundary at the ordinary high-water mark (OHWM). Texas uses a COMPUTED GRADIENT LINE at the mid-height of the qualified cut bank — requiring water-level observation, bank profiling, and gradient interpolation. Do not equate it with a simple high-water line.

content.html:
Non-navigable streams: the medium filum aquae

On a non-navigable stream there is no State bed and no gradient boundary. Title runs to the medium filum aquae — the "middle thread" or centerline of the stream. Where the two opposite banks belong to different owners, each owns to the thread; this is also expressed in federal law (43 U.S.C. §931: where opposite banks of a non-navigable stream belong to different persons, "the stream and the bed thereof shall become common to both"). The center can be defined as the center of the high-water marks, the center of the low-water marks, or the deepest channel (the thread or thalweg), depending on local law. When two adjoining deeds both call to a non-navigable stream and the channel later shifts gradually, the centerline boundary moves with it.

rows: Gradient boundary (mid-height of qualified cut bank)
Motl v. Boyd; Brainard v. State; Oklahoma v. Texas

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: The gradient boundary is the Texas limit of private title on a navigable stream: a graded line midway up the qualified cut bank, NOT the ordinary high-water mark.

It came from Oklahoma v. Texas (1923, Stiles) and was adopted for Texas public/private ownership in Motl v. Boyd (1926); refined as the mid-height of the lowest qualified bank in Brainard v. State (2000).
Motl v. Boyd defines the bed by the water's average and mean annual stage, ignoring extreme freshets and droughts.
On non-navigable streams there is no State bed: owners take to the medium filum aquae (centerline/thread/thalweg).

flashcards.definition: The Texas limit of private title on a navigable stream: a graded line along the bank midway between the lower water level reaching the cut bank and the higher level not overtopping it — the mid-height of the qualified cut bank.
The relatively permanent, water-washed bank separating the streambed from the upland, used to locate the gradient boundary.
Texas Supreme Court case adopting the gradient boundary and defining the bed by the water's average and mean annual stage, ignoring extreme freshets and droughts.
The 'middle thread' or centerline of a non-navigable stream, to which abutting owners hold title; may be the thread/thalweg (deepest channel).

images_needed.caption: Cross-section of a navigable stream bank showing the lower water level at the foot of the cut bank, the higher non-overtopping level, and the gradient boundary drawn at the mid-height of the qualified cut bank; State bed below, private upland above.
Plan view of a non-navigable stream with two opposite owners holding to the medium filum aquae (centerline/thread).

lessons.title: Tidal and Coastal Boundaries: Tides, Datums, and the Dual Standard
learning_objectives: Define the principal tidal datums and the 18.6-year tidal epoch.
Apply the Texas dual coastal rule (Luttes v. State): MHHW for Spanish/Mexican grants, mean high tide for Anglo/Texas grants.
Explain the Texas Open Beaches Act, the rolling easement, and the limit imposed by Severance v. Patterson.

content.html:
Tides and tidal datums

The Moon's gravitational pull raises tidal bulges on the near and far sides of the Earth, producing the daily ebb and flow. Because the Moon takes about 27.3 days to orbit and the tilt of its orbit cycles over 18.6 years, a complete record of tidal behavior — the tidal epoch — spans 18.6 years. Tidal boundaries are statistical: they are the averages of repeated observations over that epoch, reduced to a precise elevation (a datum). Borax Consolidated v. City of Los Angeles, 296 U.S. 10 (1935), requires the use of these scientific averaging techniques to fix a tidal boundary; it established mean high water as the Anglo-American coastal boundary.

rows: Mean High Water (MHW) / mean high tide
The average of ALL the high-water marks over the 18.6-year tidal epoch.
The average of all the low-water marks over the epoch.
The average of only the HIGHER of each day's two high tides over the epoch (a more landward line).
The average of only the lower of each day's two low tides over the epoch.

content.html:
The Texas dual coastal rule

On most American coasts the upland boundary is uniformly the mean high water line (Borax). Texas is dual. The seaward boundary of coastal upland — the line dividing private upland from State-owned tidal seabed — depends on the sovereign source of the grant:

  • Spanish/Mexican grants are bounded by the line of mean HIGHER high water (MHHW) — the more landward line. This reflects the civil law (Las Siete Partidas) that defined the shore by the reach of the highest, winter tides; the Texas Supreme Court translated that into the surveyable MHHW datum.
  • Anglo-American grants — those under the Republic of Texas or the State of Texas — are bounded by the line of mean high tide / mean high water (MHW), the ordinary high-water line.

Because MHHW lies landward of MHW, a Spanish/Mexican coastal grant yields a larger State seabed and a smaller private upland than an otherwise identical Texas-law grant.

sections.title: Luttes v. State, 159 Tex. 500, 324 S.W.2d 167 (1959)
sections.content: The controlling Texas authority on coastal upland boundaries. It concerned the 1829 Mexican "Potrero de Buena Vista" grant on the Laguna Madre. The Court held that the seaward boundary of a Spanish/Mexican grant is mean higher high water, distinguished from the mean high tide line that bounds grants made under Anglo-American (Texas) law. A coastal surveyor must therefore establish the chain of title back to the original sovereign before selecting the tidal datum.

content.text: Texas differs: The Anglo-American rule (Borax, 1935) uses mean high water everywhere. Texas is dual — mean high tide for its own-law grants, but the LANDWARD mean higher high water line for civil-law (Spanish/Mexican) grants (Luttes v. State). Always trace the chain of title to the original sovereign before choosing the coastal datum.

content.html:
The Open Beaches Act and the rolling easement

The Texas Open Beaches Act (TOBA) (originally 1959; now Tex. Nat. Res. Code Ch. 61) guarantees the public a free and unrestricted right of ingress, egress, and use of Gulf-front beaches. It recognizes a public easement over the dry beach between the line of mean low tide and the line of vegetation where a prescriptive, dedicated, or customary easement exists, and creates a prima facie presumption that this area is subject to a public easement. Historically the easement was said to "roll" landward as the vegetation line naturally migrated.

Severance v. Patterson, 370 S.W.3d 705 (Tex. 2012), limited that doctrine. A public beach easement may gradually migrate with naturally shifting beaches, but it does not automatically roll onto previously private dry land after an avulsive event (such as a hurricane suddenly moving the vegetation line landward). After avulsion, the State must establish a new easement rather than rely on the old one rolling inland. The case imports the accretion/avulsion distinction (Lesson 5) directly into beach-access law.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Tidal datums are 18.6-year-epoch averages; Borax (1935) requires scientific averaging and sets mean high water as the Anglo-American coastal boundary.
MHHW (mean higher high water) averages only the higher daily high tide and lies LANDWARD of MHW.
Texas dual rule (Luttes v. State): Spanish/Mexican grants → MHHW; Republic/State grants → mean high tide (MHW). Trace the sovereign first.
TOBA gives the public an easement over the dry beach to the vegetation line; Severance v. Patterson stops the easement from automatically rolling onto private land after an AVULSIVE event.

flashcards.definition: The 18.6-year period over which tidal observations are averaged to fix a tidal datum, reflecting the cycle of the Moon's orbital tilt.
The average of only the higher of each day's two high tides over the tidal epoch; the more landward datum used for Texas Spanish/Mexican coastal grants.
flashcards.hint_1: Higher of the two daily highs.

flashcards.definition: Texas case establishing the dual coastal datum: Spanish/Mexican grants are bounded by mean higher high water, Anglo/Texas grants by mean high tide.
Tex. Nat. Res. Code Ch. 61: guarantees public access to Gulf beaches and recognizes a public easement over the dry beach between mean low tide and the vegetation line.
Texas case holding the public beach easement may roll with gradual change but does NOT automatically roll onto private land after an avulsive event (e.g., a hurricane).

images_needed.caption: Beach profile showing MHHW, MHW, MLW, MLLW datum lines and the dry-beach zone, with the dual-rule note (MHHW for Spanish/Mexican grants, MHW for Texas grants).
Before/after panels of a beach: gradual vegetation-line migration (easement rolls) vs. avulsive hurricane shift (easement does not automatically roll) per Severance v. Patterson.

lessons.title: Shoreline Change and Apportionment of New Land
learning_objectives: Distinguish accretion, reliction, erosion, and avulsion and their differing effects on title.
Apply the rule that gradual change moves the boundary while avulsion fixes it.
Apportion newly formed riparian land among adjoining owners using the proportionate-shoreline method.

content.html:
Water boundaries that move

The general rule is that water boundaries follow the changing shoreline — but only when the change is gradual and imperceptible. The controlling variable is always gradual vs. sudden:

  • Accretion — the gradual, imperceptible accumulation of soil (alluvion) deposited by the water. The land formed belongs to the upland riparian/littoral owner, and the boundary moves outward.
  • Reliction (dereliction) — the gradual, imperceptible recession or lowering of the water, exposing land that was once covered. Treated like accretion: the new land goes to the upland owner.
  • Erosion — the gradual eating away of soil by the water. The boundary moves landward and the owner loses the eroded land (which becomes part of the bed).
  • Avulsion — the sudden, perceptible removal of soil or shift of a channel (a flood cutting a new channel, a hurricane). The boundary is FIXED at its former location; title does not change merely because the water suddenly moved.

The distinguishing feature is the time element: avulsion is sudden; accretion, reliction, and erosion are gradual.

rows: Boundary moves outward with the new shoreline
Boundary moves outward as water recedes
Upland owner loses; bed owner gains
No change of title; ownership stays put

content.text: Memory hook: GRADUAL moves the line; AVULSIVE fixes the line. An island suddenly cut off by avulsion stays with its original owner; land gradually added by accretion goes to the upland owner. Manmade/artificial accretion does NOT benefit the owner who caused it.

sections.title: Avulsion after a boundary has already moved
sections.content: If a navigable stream's boundary has been moving by accretion/erosion and then an avulsion occurs, the moment of the avulsion fixes the ownership lines. The State that owned the bed before still owns it after; the abutting owners who owned to the banks before still own to the banks after; land left under the new channel is still owned by whoever owned it before — but that owner cannot block the new channel to navigation. On a non-navigable stream, owners who held to the center before continue to hold to the center of the (now dry) former bed.

sections.title: Manmade change and re-emerged land
sections.content: Shoreline change caused directly by manmade works does not change title — a riparian owner may not benefit from accretion or avulsion that results from his own actions, and fill placed in State-owned tidelands belongs to the State. Re-emerged land: under the federal rule (followed by most states), if erosion destroys riparian lots and accretion later rebuilds them, the original owners reacquire their former rights as though never eroded; some states instead hold that the eroded lines are extinguished and the once-remote owner keeps the new accretion.

content.html:
Apportioning accretion among riparian owners

When accretion forms in front of several owners on the same side of the water, the new land — and the valuable new water frontage — must be divided with equity and justice. Two goals guide the division: each owner should get a share of the new land in proportion to their holdings, and each should keep access to the water with a share of the new waterline in proportion to their share of the old waterline. Courts caution that "no geometric theorem can be formulated to govern all cases" and look to fairness, but several standard methods exist.

The method preferred by the federal government and a majority of states is the proportionate-shoreline method (Johnston v. Jones, 66 U.S. 109 (1862); BLM Manual of Surveying Instructions). It apportions the new frontage in the same ratio as the old frontage:

content.label: Proportionate-shoreline method: each owner's new-shoreline share S_i = (owner's old frontage F_i / total old frontage) × total new shoreline length

rows: New frontage split in the same ratio as old frontage (Johnston v. Jones)
Federal/majority default; irregular or curving shorelines
Line drawn perpendicular to the thread (streams) or to the new shoreline (wider bodies)
Fairly straight shores; also divides streambed when owners own the bed
Side lines simply extended to the water
Share of accreted AREA in proportion to total frontage
When the accreted land is more valuable than the frontage (used in Louisiana)
Lines to a center point (round lake) or perpendicular to the centerline (long lake)

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Accretion and reliction (gradual gains) go to the upland owner and move the boundary; erosion (gradual loss) moves it landward; avulsion (sudden) FIXES the boundary and changes no title.
The moment of an avulsion fixes ownership; manmade/artificial change does not benefit the owner who caused it.
Newly formed riparian land is divided with equity and justice — water frontage normally controls; 'no geometric theorem governs all cases.'
The proportionate-shoreline method (Johnston v. Jones; BLM Manual) is the federal/majority default: new frontage is split in the same ratio as the old frontage.

flashcards.definition: The gradual, imperceptible accumulation of soil (alluvion) deposited by water; the new land belongs to the upland owner and the boundary moves outward.
flashcards.hint_1: Alluvion is the deposit; accretion is the process.

flashcards.definition: Dry land formed by the gradual, imperceptible recession or lowering of the water; treated like accretion (goes to the upland owner).
The gradual eating away of soil by water; the boundary moves landward and the owner loses the eroded land to the bed owner.
The sudden, perceptible removal of soil or shift of a channel; the boundary is FIXED at its former location and title does not change.
Apportionment method (Johnston v. Jones; BLM Manual) dividing new water frontage among riparian owners in the same ratio as their old frontage.

images_needed.caption: Two-panel diagram: gradual accretion moving the boundary outward (owner gains) vs. avulsive channel cut leaving the old boundary fixed and an island with its original owner.
Riparian apportionment 'fan': several lots with old frontages on the original shoreline and proportional shares projected to the longer new shoreline.

lessons.title: Water USE Law: Surface Water Appropriation vs. Groundwater Rule of Capture
learning_objectives: Explain that Texas surface water is State-owned and used only by TCEQ appropriation permit.
State the Texas rule of capture for groundwater and the ownership-in-place principle of Edwards Aquifer Authority v. Day.
Distinguish the right to a water BOUNDARY from the right to USE the water.

content.html:
Owning the bank is not owning the water

Everything so far has concerned boundaries — where the land ends and the water begins, and who owns the bed. A separate body of law governs the right to use the water itself, and in Texas the two are sharply divided. Owning the streambank does not give you a right to use the water beyond limited domestic and livestock use. Texas treats surface water and groundwater under two completely different regimes.

Surface water: State-owned, used by permit

In Texas, surface water is owned by the State — every river, natural stream, lake, bay, and arm of the Gulf (Tex. Water Code Ch. 11, esp. §11.021). A private party acquires the right to use it only by appropriation under a State-issued permit, administered by the Texas Commission on Environmental Quality (TCEQ), on a prior-appropriation basis — "first in time, first in right." A streamside landowner keeps a limited right to take water for domestic and livestock use without a permit, but anything beyond that requires a permit.

Texas historically ran a confusing riparian/appropriation hybrid. The Water Rights Adjudication Act of 1967 resolved decades of conflicting claims by merging recognized riparian claims into the single prior-appropriation permit system; claimants had to file by the deadline or have their rights folded into the unified framework.

content.text: Texas differs: Eastern states use pure riparian surface-water law; western states use prior appropriation. Texas is a historical HYBRID that was statutorily UNIFIED into appropriation in 1967. Bedrock point for surveyors and landowners: owning the streambank does NOT grant a right to use the water beyond domestic/livestock use — that requires a State (TCEQ) permit.

content.html:
Groundwater: the rule of capture and ownership in place

Groundwater follows a starkly different rule. Texas retains the rule of capture (the English "absolute ownership" rule): a landowner may pump and capture the water beneath his land — even to a neighbor's detriment — generally without liability. The rule was adopted in Houston & Texas Central Railway Co. v. East, 98 Tex. 146, 81 S.W. 279 (1904), which found no liability for draining a neighbor's well through pumping.

Modern law adds a powerful overlay. In Edwards Aquifer Authority v. Day, 369 S.W.3d 814 (Tex. 2012), the Texas Supreme Court held that a landowner owns the groundwater in place as "an exclusive and private property right ... of which he may not be deprived without a taking," protected by Tex. Const. art. I, §17 — much like oil and gas. Groundwater Conservation Districts (GCDs) may regulate pumping (Tex. Water Code Ch. 36), but regulation that cuts too deeply can be a compensable constitutional taking. The Edwards Aquifer Authority is the best-known of these districts.

content.text: Texas differs: Most states abandoned absolute capture for reasonable-use, correlative-rights, or the Restatement rule. Texas is a notable holdout that RETAINS the rule of capture while ALSO recognizing ownership in place (Edwards Aquifer Authority v. Day) — so over-regulation by a GCD can trigger takings liability.

rows: Owned by the State of Texas
Owned in place by the landowner (Day, 2012)
Rule of capture — pump what you can
Groundwater Conservation Districts (e.g., Edwards Aquifer Authority)
H&TC Ry. v. East (1904); Edwards Aquifer Authority v. Day (2012); Water Code Ch. 36

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Surface water in Texas is State-owned; use requires a TCEQ appropriation permit (prior appropriation), except limited domestic/livestock use.
The 1967 Water Rights Adjudication Act merged riparian claims into the unified appropriation permit system.
Groundwater follows the rule of capture (H&TC Ry. v. East, 1904): pump freely, generally without liability to neighbors.
Edwards Aquifer Authority v. Day (2012): the landowner owns groundwater in place; GCD over-regulation can be a compensable taking.

flashcards.definition: Under Tex. Water Code §11.021, all surface water in Texas (rivers, streams, lakes, bays, arms of the Gulf) is owned by the State; use requires a TCEQ appropriation permit.
The 'first in time, first in right' system by which Texas allocates the right to USE State-owned surface water through TCEQ permits.
The Texas rule that a landowner may pump and capture groundwater beneath his land, even to a neighbor's harm, generally without liability (H&TC Ry. v. East, 1904).
Texas case holding a landowner owns groundwater in place as a vested, constitutionally protected property right; over-regulation by a GCD can be a compensable taking.

images_needed.caption: Split schematic contrasting State-owned surface water allocated by TCEQ appropriation permits with landowner-owned groundwater governed by the rule of capture and GCDs.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best describes the difference between riparian and littoral rights?
options: Riparian rights attach to flowing watercourses (rivers/streams); littoral rights attach to static or tidal bodies (lakes, sea, Gulf).
Riparian rights attach to the ocean; littoral rights attach to rivers.
Riparian rights are public; littoral rights are private.
There is no legal difference; the terms are perfect synonyms.
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Riparian rights attach to flowing watercourses (rivers/streams); littoral rights attach to static or tidal bodies (lakes, sea, Gulf).
quiz_questions.explanation: Riparian (Latin ripa, river bank) concerns moving water; littoral (Latin litus, seashore) concerns static/tidal water. The other options invert or deny the distinction; although 'riparian' is loosely used for both, they are not legally identical.

quiz_questions.question_text: A back lot in a subdivision does not touch the lake but lies one lot behind a waterfront lot. Erosion later destroys the waterfront lot, leaving the back lot bordering the lake. What is true of the back lot's riparian rights?
options: It had no riparian rights until it became riparian by the erosion; rights attach only once a parcel actually borders the water.
It always had riparian rights because it was in a waterfront subdivision.
It can never acquire riparian rights because it was not originally riparian.
Riparian rights are personal and cannot attach to land at all.
quiz_questions.correct_answer: It had no riparian rights until it became riparian by the erosion; rights attach only once a parcel actually borders the water.
quiz_questions.explanation: Riparian/littoral rights attach only to a parcel that actually has a water boundary. The back lot acquires those rights when it becomes riparian, not before; it does not keep or forever lack them based on its original position.

quiz_questions.question_text: Under the equal-footing doctrine, a state admitted after 1789 takes title to the beds of its navigable waters as of the date of statehood.
options: True
False
quiz_questions.correct_answer: True
quiz_questions.explanation: Pollard v. Hagan (1845): new states enter on an equal footing with the original states and take bed title to navigable waters at statehood, subject to federal regulation of commerce.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why must a Texas surveyor identify the original sovereign and date of a coastal or streamside grant before choosing a water-boundary rule?
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Because Texas land traces to four sovereigns (Spain, Mexico, Republic, State) and was never federal public domain; the boundary rule — especially the coastal datum — can differ depending on which sovereign issued the grant.
quiz_questions.explanation: Texas retained its public lands at annexation and traces title to Spain, Mexico, the Republic, or the State. The coastal datum (and other rules) turn on the sovereign source — e.g., Spanish/Mexican grants use mean higher high water while Texas grants use mean high tide.

quiz_questions.question_text: Under Tex. Nat. Res. Code §21.001(3), a stream is statutorily 'navigable' if it:
options: Retains an average width of 30 feet, measured across the bed, from its mouth up.
Carries flowing water at least 30 days per year.
Is at least 30 feet deep at its mouth.
Has been declared navigable by the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers.
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Retains an average width of 30 feet, measured across the bed, from its mouth up.
quiz_questions.explanation: Section 21.001(3) defines a navigable stream by average bed width of 30 feet from the mouth up — a geometric test measured bank to bank, not by days of flow, depth, or a federal declaration.

quiz_questions.question_text: A Texas creek is dry for several months each year but, measured cut bank to cut bank, averages 34 feet wide from its mouth up. Under Texas law, who owns the bed?
options: The State of Texas, because the creek is statutorily navigable (≥30 ft average bed width) despite being seasonally dry.
The abutting owners to the center, because the creek is dry part of the year.
The federal government, because navigability is a federal question.
No one; a seasonally dry creek has no bed.
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The State of Texas, because the creek is statutorily navigable (≥30 ft average bed width) despite being seasonally dry.
quiz_questions.explanation: The 30-foot test measures the bed, not the water present on a given day. A seasonally dry channel averaging ≥30 ft is statutorily navigable, so the State owns the bed (subject to the Small Bill's surface relinquishment).

quiz_questions.question_text: The 1929 Small Bill relinquished the State's interest in navigable streambed surface to adjoining owners but reserved the minerals to the State.
options: True
False
quiz_questions.correct_answer: True
quiz_questions.explanation: The Small Bill (Art. 5414a) quitclaimed the streambed SURFACE to adjoining owners and let them make up short acreage, but it reserved minerals/sand/gravel to the State and preserved public water rights.

quiz_questions.question_text: On a non-navigable Texas stream whose opposite banks are owned by different people, how far does each owner's title extend?
options: To the center (medium filum aquae / thread) of the channel.
Only to the top of the bank on their side.
To the gradient boundary on their side.
Nowhere into the channel; the State owns the entire bed.
quiz_questions.correct_answer: To the center (medium filum aquae / thread) of the channel.
quiz_questions.explanation: On non-navigable streams there is no State-owned bed; each abutting owner takes to the centerline/thread (43 U.S.C. §931). The gradient boundary and State bed apply only to navigable streams.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Texas gradient boundary on a navigable stream is best described as:
options: A graded line at the mid-height of the qualified cut bank, midway between the lower water level reaching the bank and the higher level not overtopping it.
The ordinary high-water mark identified by the vegetation line.
The thread or thalweg of the deepest channel.
The line of mean higher high water.
quiz_questions.correct_answer: A graded line at the mid-height of the qualified cut bank, midway between the lower water level reaching the bank and the higher level not overtopping it.
quiz_questions.explanation: Texas uses a computed gradient line at the mid-height of the qualified cut bank (Motl v. Boyd; Brainard v. State) — not the OHWM (used in most states), not the thalweg (non-navigable streams), and not MHHW (a coastal datum).

quiz_questions.question_text: Most U.S. states fix the riparian boundary at the ordinary high-water mark, but Texas instead uses the gradient boundary.
options: True
False
quiz_questions.correct_answer: True
quiz_questions.explanation: The gradient boundary — a computed line at the mid-height of the qualified cut bank — is distinctively Texas. Virtually every other state uses the observable ordinary high-water mark.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which case originated the gradient boundary method, and in what dispute?
options: Oklahoma v. Texas (1923) — the Red River boundary, where surveyor A.A. Stiles developed the method.
Luttes v. State (1959) — a Laguna Madre coastal grant.
Severance v. Patterson (2012) — a Galveston beach easement.
The Daniel Ball (1870) — a federal commerce navigability case.
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Oklahoma v. Texas (1923) — the Red River boundary, where surveyor A.A. Stiles developed the method.
quiz_questions.explanation: Oklahoma v. Texas, 260 U.S. 606 (1923), fixed the Texas–Oklahoma line on the Red River; Stiles developed the gradient boundary there. Texas adopted it for public/private ownership in Motl v. Boyd (1926). The other cases concern coastal datums, beach easements, and federal navigability.

quiz_questions.question_text: In Motl v. Boyd, the 'bed' of a navigable stream is defined by the water's average and mean annual stage. What does this exclude?
options: Extreme freshets (floods) and extreme droughts.
All flow except the single highest flood of record.
Any flow below mean low water.
Only manmade diversions.
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Extreme freshets (floods) and extreme droughts.
quiz_questions.explanation: Motl v. Boyd defines the bed as the soil the water occupies at its average and mean annual stage, expressly disregarding extreme freshets and extreme droughts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Mean higher high water (MHHW) is computed by averaging:
options: Only the higher of each day's two high tides over the tidal epoch.
All high tides over the tidal epoch.
The lower of each day's two low tides.
The midpoint of each day's range.
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Only the higher of each day's two high tides over the tidal epoch.
quiz_questions.explanation: MHHW averages only the higher daily high; MHW averages ALL highs. Because it ignores the lower daily highs, MHHW lies landward of MHW.

quiz_questions.question_text: A coastal Texas tract derives from an 1829 Mexican grant. Under Luttes v. State, what datum bounds it on the seaward side, and is the private upland larger or smaller than under a State-of-Texas grant?
options: Mean higher high water; the private upland is smaller (the State seabed is larger).
Mean high tide; the private upland is larger.
Mean lower low water; the private upland is larger.
Mean high water; identical to a State grant.
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Mean higher high water; the private upland is smaller (the State seabed is larger).
quiz_questions.explanation: Luttes: Spanish/Mexican grants are bounded by MHHW (the landward line), so the private upland is smaller and the State seabed larger than under a Texas grant bounded by mean high tide (MHW).

quiz_questions.question_text: The tidal epoch over which tidal datums are averaged is:
options: 18.6 years.
27.3 days.
12.42 hours.
100 years.
quiz_questions.correct_answer: 18.6 years.
quiz_questions.explanation: The 18.6-year epoch reflects the cycle of the Moon's orbital tilt. 27.3 days is the orbital period and 12.42 hours is the interval between high and low tides, but the averaging epoch is 18.6 years.

quiz_questions.question_text: Under Severance v. Patterson, a public beach easement automatically rolls landward onto previously private dry land immediately after a hurricane shifts the vegetation line.
options: True
False
quiz_questions.correct_answer: False
quiz_questions.explanation: Severance held the easement may roll with GRADUAL change but does NOT automatically roll onto private land after an AVULSIVE event (e.g., a hurricane); the State must establish a new easement.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two lots, B and E, both call to a non-navigable river that forms their common boundary. Over years the channel shifts gradually. What happens to the common boundary?
options: The boundary moves with the channel; gradual accretion/erosion shifts the centerline boundary.
The boundary stays fixed at the original channel location.
The State takes ownership of the abandoned channel.
Both lots lose all river frontage.
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The boundary moves with the channel; gradual accretion/erosion shifts the centerline boundary.
quiz_questions.explanation: Gradual, imperceptible change moves a water boundary. With the river as the common (centerline) boundary, the line follows the slowly shifting channel.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two lots, B and E, both call to a river forming their common boundary. During a single flood the river suddenly cuts a new channel (an avulsion). What happens to the common boundary?
options: The boundary is fixed at the river's former location; avulsion does not change title or move the boundary.
The boundary instantly relocates to the new channel.
Ownership of both lots transfers to the State.
The lots must be re-platted and re-deeded.
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The boundary is fixed at the river's former location; avulsion does not change title or move the boundary.
quiz_questions.explanation: Avulsion is sudden and perceptible: the boundary is fixed at the former channel location and title does not change, in contrast to gradual change which moves the line.

quiz_questions.question_text: An island suddenly forms in a stream during a flood (avulsion). Who owns it?
options: Whoever owned that bed/soil immediately before the avulsion; sudden change does not transfer title.
The upland riparian owner nearest the island, as accretion.
Always the State, regardless of stream class.
It becomes unowned public domain.
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Whoever owned that bed/soil immediately before the avulsion; sudden change does not transfer title.
quiz_questions.explanation: Because avulsion does not change ownership, the island belongs to whoever owned that soil before the event — e.g., the bed owner (the State on a navigable stream, or the center owners on a non-navigable stream). It is not treated as accretion to an upland owner.

quiz_questions.question_text: Land gradually added to the upland by the imperceptible deposit of soil (alluvion) belongs to the upland riparian owner.
options: True
False
quiz_questions.correct_answer: True
quiz_questions.explanation: Accretion — gradual, imperceptible deposit of alluvion — inures to the upland owner and moves the boundary outward.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which apportionment method is the federal/majority default for dividing newly accreted land among riparian owners?
options: The proportionate-shoreline method (Johnston v. Jones).
The prolongation-of-property-line method.
The proportionate-acreage method (Louisiana).
The round-lake (pie) method.
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The proportionate-shoreline method (Johnston v. Jones).
quiz_questions.explanation: The proportionate-shoreline method, recommended by the BLM Manual and used by the federal government and a majority of states, splits the new frontage in the same ratio as the old. The others are situational alternatives.

quiz_questions.question_text: A riparian owner builds a jetty that causes sand to accumulate (artificial accretion) in front of his lot. Does he gain title to that new land?
quiz_questions.correct_answer: No. A riparian owner may not benefit from accretion (or avulsion) that results from his own manmade actions; manmade shoreline change does not change title in his favor.
quiz_questions.explanation: Shoreline change resulting directly from the owner's own manmade works does not work an ownership change in his favor; the rule rewarding accretion applies to natural deposition, not self-induced fill.

quiz_questions.question_text: In Texas, who owns surface water (rivers, lakes, bays), and how does a private party get the right to use it?
options: The State owns it; a private party uses it only by a TCEQ appropriation permit (with limited domestic/livestock use exempt).
The adjoining landowner owns it outright and may use it freely.
The federal government owns it; the EPA issues use permits.
It is unowned; anyone may take any amount.
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The State owns it; a private party uses it only by a TCEQ appropriation permit (with limited domestic/livestock use exempt).
quiz_questions.explanation: Tex. Water Code §11.021: surface water is State-owned; use requires a TCEQ appropriation permit under prior appropriation, except limited domestic/livestock use. Owning the bank does not grant a use right.

quiz_questions.question_text: Texas groundwater follows the rule of capture, and under Edwards Aquifer Authority v. Day the landowner also owns the groundwater in place.
options: True
False
quiz_questions.correct_answer: True
quiz_questions.explanation: Texas retains the rule of capture (H&TC Ry. v. East, 1904) and Day (2012) recognizes ownership in place as a vested, constitutionally protected right; over-regulation by a GCD can be a compensable taking.

quiz_questions.question_text: A landowner pumps so heavily that his neighbor's well goes dry. Under the Texas rule of capture, is he liable to the neighbor?
options: Generally no — under the rule of capture a landowner may capture groundwater beneath his land even to a neighbor's detriment, without liability.
Yes — Texas uses the reasonable-use rule, which forbids harming a neighbor.
Yes — pumping that dries a neighbor's well is automatically a taking.
Only if the neighbor's well predates his by 10 years (prior appropriation).
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Generally no — under the rule of capture a landowner may capture groundwater beneath his land even to a neighbor's detriment, without liability.
quiz_questions.explanation: H&TC Ry. v. East adopted the rule of capture; generally there is no liability for draining a neighbor's well by pumping. Prior appropriation governs SURFACE water, not groundwater, and reasonable-use is the rule Texas notably rejected for groundwater.

problem_templates.name: Statutory navigability of a Texas stream from a vara width
problem_templates.question_template: An original GLO field note reports the average bed width of a stream, cut bank to cut bank, as {{wvrs:f2}} varas. Convert this average width to feet to apply the Texas statutory navigability test of Tex. Nat. Res. Code §21.001(3). (1 vara = 33⅓ in = 2.77778 ft.) What is the average bed width in feet?
problem_templates.explanation_template: Convert varas to feet (× 2.77778): {{wvrs:f2}} vrs = {{_answer}} ft. Under §21.001(3) the stream is statutorily navigable (State-owned bed, gradient boundary) only if this average bed width is at least 30 ft; otherwise it is non-navigable and owners take to the center thread.

images_needed.caption: Stream bed cross-section with average cut-bank-to-cut-bank width labeled in varas and converted to feet, with the 30-ft threshold marked.

problem_templates.name: Apportioning new shoreline by proportionate-shoreline method
problem_templates.question_template: Three riparian owners share one side of a slowly accreting shore. Their ORIGINAL frontages on the old waterline are A = {{fa:f2}} ft, B = {{fb:f2}} ft, and C = {{fc:f2}} ft. Accretion has lengthened the NEW waterline to {{lnew:f2}} ft total. Using the proportionate-shoreline method (new frontage split in the same ratio as old frontage), how many feet of the NEW waterline are allotted to owner B?
problem_templates.explanation_template: The proportionate-shoreline method (Johnston v. Jones; BLM Manual) gives each owner the same share of the NEW waterline as of the OLD. B's share = (B's frontage ÷ total old frontage) × new waterline = ({{fb:f2}} ÷ {{ftot:f2}}) × {{lnew:f2}} = {{_answer}} ft.

images_needed.caption: Apportionment fan: three old frontages A, B, C on the original shoreline, with proportional shares projected outward to the longer new shoreline.

problem_templates.name: Area of accreted (riparian) land along a frontage
problem_templates.question_template: Gradual accretion has built a band of new land in front of a riparian lot. The lot's water frontage is {{front:f2}} ft and the new alluvion extends an average of {{depth:f2}} ft outward from the old waterline to the new one. Treating the band as frontage × average depth, how many ACRES of accreted land has the riparian owner gained? (1 acre = 43,560 sq ft.)
problem_templates.explanation_template: Accretion gradually added land that belongs to the upland owner. Area = frontage × average accretion depth = {{front:f2}} × {{depth:f2}} = {{area_sf:f2}} sq ft = {{_answer}} acres (÷ 43,560).

images_needed.caption: Plan view of a riparian lot with its water frontage and the average outward depth of the accreted band, shaded as the new land.

homework.title: Homework 1 — Fisherman Acres: Water Boundaries and Shoreline Change
homework.prompt:
Use the Fisherman Acres Subdivision diagram (provided) to answer the problems below. **Answer ONLY FIVE of the six problems (1–6).** Each is worth 2 points: one point for the answer, one for the explanation. For each, state the rule and apply it to the facts.

> **Authority note.** Apply general water-boundary doctrine. For any problem turning on a code rule, you may reference **California Civil Code §§670 and 830** as a comparison model: §670 — the State owns land below ordinary high-water mark bordering tidewater and below a navigable lake or stream; §830 — absent contrary grant intent, the upland owner takes to ordinary high-water mark on tidewater, to low-water mark on a non-tidal navigable lake/stream, and to the **middle** of any other (non-navigable) water. **Then contrast the result with the Texas rule** (gradient boundary on navigable streams; medium filum aquae on non-navigable streams; State-owned navigable beds) so you can see how the choice of jurisdiction changes the boundary.

1. The deeds for **Lot B** and **Lot E** both call to **Salmon River**. If the channel **gradually** changed location, what effect, if any, would this have on the common boundary of the two lots? Briefly explain.

2. The deeds for **Lot B** and **Lot E** both call to **Salmon River**. The channel changed location during the **1997 'El Niño' flood** (an avulsive event). What effect, if any, would this have on the common boundary? Briefly explain.

3. The deed for **Lot A** calls to **Bass Bay**. Describe the **northerly** property line of Lot A. Briefly explain.

4. The deed for **Lot C** calls to **Trout Lake**. Describe the **southerly** boundary of Lot C. Briefly explain.

5. The deeds for **Lot C** and **Lot D** both call to **Bear Creek**. Describe the **easterly** boundary of Lot C. Briefly explain.

6. If **Pike Island** was formed during the **1997 'El Niño' flood**, who would own the island? Briefly explain.

**In your write-up, for every answer add one sentence stating how the result would change (or not) if the parcel were in Texas** — e.g., a navigable Salmon River bed would be State-owned with the private boundary at the gradient boundary, and an avulsively formed Pike Island would stay with the pre-flood bed owner.

images_needed.caption: Fisherman Acres Subdivision plat: Lots A–E around Bass Bay, Trout Lake, Salmon River, and Bear Creek, with Pike Island in the river; lot lines call to the water features.

homework.title: Homework 2 — Parcels A–G: Describing the Water Boundary Line
homework.prompt:
Use the parcel diagram (provided). **For only FIVE of parcels A through G**, (i) **describe the water boundary line** of that parcel, and (ii) **where applicable, describe any limitations on ownership** (for example, a State-owned bed, a public-trust easement, public navigation rights, a mineral reservation, or a use-permit requirement). 2 points each.

For each parcel you choose, structure your answer as:

- **Classify the water.** Riparian (flowing stream) or littoral (lake/sea/Gulf)? Navigable or non-navigable? Tidal or non-tidal?
- **Locate the boundary.** State the exact line (e.g., **medium filum aquae** / center thread for a non-navigable stream; the **gradient boundary** for a navigable Texas stream; **mean high tide** for a Texas tidal grant or **mean higher high water** for a Spanish/Mexican tidal grant; low-water or high-water mark as the diagram and any stated jurisdiction require).
- **Note ownership limits.** E.g., the State owns the bed of a navigable stream (public trust); the 1929 Small Bill may have relinquished the bed surface but reserved minerals and public water rights; a Gulf-front parcel carries a TOBA public-beach easement to the vegetation line; surface-water USE requires a TCEQ appropriation permit even though the bank is privately owned.

Write each description as you would for an opinion to a client's attorney: rule, application to the parcel, and conclusion.

images_needed.caption: Diagram of parcels A–G fronting a mix of water features (a navigable river, a non-navigable creek, a lake, a tidal bay/Gulf shore), each with a deed call to its water boundary.
